examples.title: FIFO Page Replacement Algorithm Simulation
examples.description: Python program to simulate FIFO page replacement algorithm and count page faults
examples.code:
def fifo_page_replacement(pages, frames):
    memory = []  # Stores pages currently in memory
    page_faults = 0
    page_table = {}
    
    for i, page in enumerate(pages):
        print(f"Step {i+1}: Reference Page {page}")
        
        if page not in memory:
            # Page fault occurs
            page_faults += 1
            print(f"  -> PAGE FAULT #{page_faults}")
            
            if len(memory) < frames:
                # Free frame available
                memory.append(page)
            else:
                # Replace oldest page (FIFO)
                replaced = memory.pop(0)
                print(f"  -> Replacing page {replaced}")
                memory.append(page)
        else:
            print(f"  -> Page already in memory")
        
        print(f"  Memory state: {memory}\n")
    
    return page_faults

# Test with reference string
pages = [7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2]
frames = 3
faults = fifo_page_replacement(pages, frames)
print(f"Total Page Faults: {faults}")
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates FIFO page replacement by maintaining a queue of pages in memory. When a page fault occurs and memory is full, the oldest page (first in queue) is replaced. For the given reference string with 3 frames, FIFO produces 9 page faults.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) where n = number of references, f = number of frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for storing frames in memory

examples.title: LRU Page Replacement Algorithm in C
examples.description: C program implementing Least Recently Used page replacement algorithm
examples.explanation: This C program implements LRU page replacement. It maintains a time counter and tracks when each page was last used. When replacement is needed, the page with the smallest time value (least recently used) is selected. LRU typically performs better than FIFO but requires hardware support for accurate implementation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) where n = page references, f = number of frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for frame array and time tracking

examples.title: Disk Scheduling - SCAN Algorithm Pseudocode
examples.description: Pseudocode for SCAN disk scheduling algorithm (Elevator Algorithm)
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SCAN(requests, start_head, max_cylinder)
BEGIN
    // requests: array of cylinder numbers to service
    // start_head: initial position of disk head
    // max_cylinder: maximum cylinder number
    
    SET left_requests = []
    SET right_requests = []
    SET current_head = start_head
    SET total_seek_time = 0
    SET service_direction = "right"
    
    // Separate requests into left and right of start head
    FOR EACH request IN requests DO
        IF request < current_head THEN
            ADD request TO left_requests
        ELSE
            ADD request TO right_requests
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    // Sort both lists in ascending order
    SORT left_requests IN DESCENDING ORDER
    SORT right_requests IN ASCENDING ORDER
    
    // Service requests in SCAN order
    // First, service all requests to the right
    FOR EACH request IN right_requests DO
        seek_distance = ABS(current_head - request)
        total_seek_time = total_seek_time + seek_distance
        current_head = request
        PRINT "Servicing request at cylinder: " + request
    END FOR
    
    // Go to end of disk if not already there
    IF current_head < max_cylinder THEN
        total_seek_time = total_seek_time + (max_cylinder - current_head)
        current_head = max_cylinder
    END IF
    
    // Reverse direction and service left requests
    FOR EACH request IN left_requests DO
        seek_distance = ABS(current_head - request)
        total_seek_time = total_seek_time + seek_distance
        current_head = request
        PRINT "Servicing request at cylinder: " + request
    END FOR
    
    PRINT "Total Seek Time: " + total_seek_time
    RETURN total_seek_time
END
examples.explanation: SCAN algorithm works like an elevator. The head moves in one direction servicing all requests until it reaches the end, then reverses direction. It provides good performance with reduced starvation compared to SSTF.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting the request queue
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing split request queues

examples.title: Page Table Entry and Address Translation
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how virtual address is translated to physical address using page table
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates virtual address translation in a paged memory system. The virtual address is divided into page number and offset. The page number indexes into the page table to find the corresponding frame number, which is combined with the offset to form the physical address.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for TLB hit, O(n) for page table lookup without TLB
